one.en: PRACTICE AND PROCEDURE – Joinder – Where the wife seeks to join third parties, including individuals and corporate entities – Where the putative respo…

summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PRACTICE AND PROCEDURE – Joinder – Where the wife seeks to join third parties, including individuals and corporate entities – Where the putative respondents opposed the joinder – Where the wife contends that the putative respondents hold interests in companies the subject of the proceedings – Where the Court is satisfied that the rights of the putative respondents may be directly affected by the orders sought – Orders made for joinder of the putative respondents. FAMILY LAW – PRACTICE AND PROCEDURE – Joinder – Where the husband seeks to join the wife’s parents to the proceedings in relation to properties held in their names – Where the husband seeks declaratory relief only and no orders which would affect the ownership of those properties – Where the Court is not satisfied that they are necessary parties and consequently there is no basis for their joinder – Application dismissed.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

SCHONELL J:

By Application in a Proceeding filed on 11 February 2026, the wife seeks to join to the proceedings Mr R, Mr T and three companies, namely, C Pty Ltd Entity 3 (“[Entity 3]”), C Pty Ltd Entity 4 (“[Entity 4]”) and C Pty Ltd Entity 5 (“[Entity 5]”).
The husband, by Application in a Proceeding filed on 6 March 2026, seeks to join to the proceedings Mr U and Mrs V, the parents of the wife.
On 16 March 2026, each of the proposed joinder parties (except for the corporate entities) appeared and verbalised their opposition to joinder. On that occasion, orders were made to facilitate determination of the applications as follows:
Mr [R], a proposed joinder party to these proceedings, is to file and serve by no later than 4.00 pm on 20 March 2026 any evidence upon which he intends to rely in opposition to the joinder application together with a three-page written submission as to why he should not be joined to the proceedings.
Mr [T], who is also a proposed joinder party, is to file and serve by no later than 4.00 pm on 20 March 2026 any affidavit on which he intends to rely in opposition to the joinder application together with a three-page written submission as to why he should not be joined to the proceedings.
The wife is to file and serve any written submissions in reply by no later than 4.00 pm next Tuesday, 24 March 2026.
I otherwise stand the matter over for determination in chambers.
In relation to the husband’s Application in a Proceeding filed 6 March 2026 seeking the joinder [Mr U] and [Mrs V], each of those persons is to file and serve by no later than 4.00 pm on 30 March 2026 any affidavit evidence on which they intend to rely supported by written submissions of no more than three pages each.
The husband is to file and serve in support of his application, written submissions by no later than 4.00 pm on 20 March 2026.
I otherwise stand the matter over for determination in chambers.
Mr R and Mr T filed their affidavits in accordance with the above orders but failed to file any written submission. The wife filed written submissions in accordance with the orders. The husband failed to comply with the orders to file written submissions by 20 March 2026, instead filing written submissions on 23 March 2026. Mr U and Mrs V, while filing affidavits in accordance with the orders, did not file any written submissions. Documents filed outside of the time prescribed by the orders have been ignored.
Persons who fail to comply with court orders cannot be heard to complain when determinations are made on the basis of documents filed in accordance with those directions.

BACKGROUND

The husband, wife and others are engaged in proceedings pursuant to Pt VIII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”), arising from the breakdown of their marriage. The proceedings are listed for a five-day final hearing commencing on 28 September 2026. Several entities are already joined to the proceedings.
The wife contends that the joinder of Mr R is necessary, as he is a director of Entity 2, Entity 4 and Entity 5, a shareholder in Entity 4 and Entity 2, and a party named in various loans. Part of the wife’s final relief seeks orders in relation to Entity 2 and s 106B applications. In that respect, in the Application in a Proceeding, she records relief more properly to be sought at a final hearing as follows:
A declaration that the Applicant Husband's convening of a shareholders meeting with respect to [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 2]ACN [...] was void and of no effect for the purpose of section 249H of the Corporations Act 2001.
A declaration that the resolution sought at the shareholder's meeting with respect to [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 2] ACN [...] for the removal of [Mr S], and calling of the meeting for that purpose, was in breach of section 249H(3) of the Corporations Act 2001 where notice was not given as to the intention to remove a director and the shareholders agreement at clause 12.3 provides for removal of directors 'in accordance with the constitution and the relevant laws'.
A declaration that the Applicant Husband's appointment of himself and Mr [R] as directors of [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 2] was in breach of the Shareholders Agreement for [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 2] ACN [...] and / or section 203C of the Corporations Act and is void and of no effect at law.
...
An order that the register of members of [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 2] ACN [...]; [J] Pty Ltd ACN [...]; and [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 1] ACN [...] be rectified so as to record register as maintained before the transactions referred to in the preceding orders.
An order that the register of directors of [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 2] ACN [...]; [J] Pty Ltd ACN [...]; and [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 1] ACN [...] be rectified so as to record register as maintained before the transactions referred to in the preceding orders.
An order that the Australian Securities and Investments Commission rectify the public register maintained by it in respect of [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 2] ACN [...]; [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 1] ACN [...]; and [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 1] ACN [...] following upon the orders made in the preceding paragraphs.
In that respect, the wife’s affidavit in support contends as follows:
J Pty Ltd
On [...] March 2018, [J] Pty Ltd was registered. I was the sole Director and Secretary from its registration until March 2025, when I was removed. I was not aware that I had been removed until searches were recently undertaken as set out in this affidavit.
There are (and has at all material times been) 100 ordinary shares valued at $1 each issued in [J Pty Ltd].
I owned all of the shares in [J Pty Ltd] from its inception in 2018 until [...] February 2025. Despite me being removed as the sole shareholder and director of [J Pty Ltd], I have not previously consented to my removal. I was not aware that I had been removed as a director and shareholder of [J Pty Ltd] until October 2025, as described below.

Shares owned in [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 2] (Third Defendant)
[J Pty Ltd] owned a 50% shareholding in [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 2]. The balance of the shares is owned by [W Pty] Ltd.
[J Pty Ltd] also owns shareholding in [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 5], [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 4]and [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 3].
After attending court on 10 October 2025, I caused my solicitor to undertake updated searches of some of the corporate entities.
These searches revealed that the following had occurred without my knowledge, approval or consent which shows that [Mr Fry] was dealing with the corporate entities without my knowledge:
On [early] February 2025, [Mr Fry] ceased being the sole director and secretary of [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 1]. [Mr B Fry], [Mr Fry’s] father, became the sole director and secretary and changed the registered address of [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 1] to [Mr B Fry’s] home address. [Mr B Fry] is recorded as having purchased [Mr Fry’s] beneficially held 100% shareholding in [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 1]. The ASIC Form 484 is annexed and marked "A".
On [later] February 2025, [Mr B Fry], as Director of [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 2], notified ASIC that the 50% shareholding of [J Pty Ltd] in [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 2] had been transferred to [Entity 1] for consideration of $1,743. The ASIC Form 484 is annexed and marked "B".
On [...] March 2025, [Mr B Fry], as Director of [Entity 3], notified ASIC that the 900 shares owned by [J Pty Ltd] had been transferred to [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 1] for consideration of $90. The ASIC Form 484 is annexed and marked "C".

...

17. Prior to these transactions referred to at paragraph 13 above:
a. I was the 100% beneficial shareholder of [J Pty Ltd]; and

b. [J Pty Ltd] owned the following:
i. 50% shareholding in [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 2];

ii. 50% shareholding in [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 4];
50% shareholding in [C] Pty Ltd[Entity 5]; and
900 of the 1,125 issued shares in [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 3].
I was not notified about any of these transactions. I did not sign any documents as the Director of [J Pty Ltd]. There was no money paid to me or to [J Pty Ltd] for any of the transfers (including the transfer of shares in [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 2]), nor did I receive any money as the 100% beneficial shareholder of the [J Pty Ltd].
I have an extant application in these Proceedings to set aside the transactions regarding [J Pty Ltd].
Transfer of shares in [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 2] at significant undervalue
The shares now held by [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 1] in [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 2] (the Second Defendant) - which had been beneficially owned by me until the purported transfer - were valued at $458,257. This was based on information [Mr Fry] supplied to the single expert Nicholas Gaudion, appointed to value our corporate assets.
Whilst I do not accept this to be an accurate valuation of my shares, this value is significantly greater than the $1,743 for which [Mr B Fry] and/or [Mr Fry] have purported to transfer the shares, as reflected in Annexure C.

Purported shareholders meeting on 2 February 2025
At 9.35 pm on Friday, [...] January 2026, [...], [Mr Fry] called a shareholders' meeting of [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 2].
I became aware of this meeting on 27 January 2026, being the first day of the final hearing in these Proceedings. [Mr Fry] was not, at the time, a director or a shareholder. I believe that he had no capacity to call that meeting.

...
As a result of these conversations, I caused my solicitor to undertake an updating search of [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 2]. Annexed hereto and marked with the letter "H" is a true copy of that search. The search shows that [Mr S] has been removed as a director and that [Mr Fry] and [Mr R] have been installed as directors of the company.

...
I am extremely concerned because [Mr Fry] is now solely operating the [Entity 2 business] with [Mr R] and they are taking the clients from [Entity 4] and [Entity 5] to [Entity 2]. This puts me in breach of the [AA Finance Company] loan conditions. I am a personal guarantee of both of these loans, which I believe to be approximately $800,000. These loan agreements are annexed above and marked "I".

(Affidavit of [Ms Petty] filed 11 February 2026)
In relation to Mr T, the wife asserts that he is the trustee for the other shareholders of C Pty Ltd Entity 3 (who is the putative eighth respondent) and contends that orders are sought in relation to the shareholding that would affect him.
Mr T, in his affidavit filed 20 March 2026, states:
I am the Supervising Tax agent for [C] Pty Ltd [Entity 3] (Tax Agents Number [...]).
In my role as Director, I am responsible for the day-to-day management and operational decision of The Company.
The Company operates as a small accounting business and currently employs two full-time staff members.
...
I do not take any position in the dispute between the Applicant and the Respondent and do not express any opinion regarding the outcome of these proceedings.
I am not a party to these proceedings and have no involvement in the personal dispute between the Applicant and the Respondent. I respectfully provide this affidavit only to assist the Court with factual information relating to the Company.
Mr R, in his affidavit filed 20 March 2026, states:
I am the sole director of C Pty Ltd Entity 5 ("[Entity 5]") and C Pty Ltd Entity 4 ("[Entity 4]").
...
[Entity 5] provides tax and accounting services to individuals and small to medium-sized businesses. As sole director, I am responsible for the day-to-day operations of the business, including:

...
I am taking steps to protect the assets and ongoing viability of the businesses for the benefit of all stakeholders.

...
To my knowledge, [Ms Petty] remains a beneficiary of the relevant family trust. I understand that changes to trustees or directors do not remove her status as a beneficiary.
I am not aware of any communication from Ms Jo-Anna Moy or her client correcting or withdrawing statements which I believe to be inaccurate or misleading, whether to the Court, financial institutions, or any other parties.
I say that the continued existence of such statements is causing disruption, uncertainty, and reputational harm to the business.
l have instructed my corporate solicitors to issue correspondence seeking that certain statements made about me and the business be withdrawn and corrected, and not further disseminated.

...
I am endeavouring to continue operating the business for the benefit of all stakeholders, including [Mr Fry] and [Ms Petty].
Based on the material filed on behalf of [Ms Petty], I am concerned that there appears to be a misunderstanding or mischaracterisation of key concepts, including trusts, beneficial ownership, director disqualification, and the regulatory requirements applicable to tax agents.
I say that I am doing my best to preserve the value of the business assets for the benefit of all stakeholders.
Further, the assertion that shares and/or beneficial ownership have been transferred to [Mr B Fry] is, to the best of my knowledge, incorrect and unsupported.

...
If I am joined as a party to these proceedings, I will be required to divert significant time and resources away from the operation of the business.
In my view, such diversion will materially impact the performance and value of the business.
In those circumstances, I may be placed in a position where I have no practical alternative but to consider resigning as director of [Entity 5] and [Entity 4] and ceasing my professional registration.
I say that such an outcome would have a significant adverse impact on the businesses and the value of the matrimonial asset pool.
The wife’s written submissions filed 24 March 2026 identify the following matters as relevant to the joinder application:
Mr R – Shareholder and Director of C Pty Ltd Entity 4 and C Pty Ltd Entity 5
At the date of preparing his Single Expert Report, the Applicant Husband and Respondent Wife’s interest in [C Pty Ltd Entity 4] and [C Pty Ltd Entity 5] were held by the [Petty Fry] Trust, namely 50 of 100 ordinary shares. Mr [R] was recorded as holding the remaining 50 ordinary shares.

...
Mr [R] refers to “The relevant loan facilities... guaranteed primarily by the company, with additional guarantees provided by other parties, including myself and related individuals...”. Further, Mr [R] indicates that he has “communicated with [AA Finance Company] regarding a proposed three-month moratorium on repayments”. The Respondent Wife is a guarantor of the loan facility referred to and specific orders are sought in the Application in a Proceeding filed 12 February 2026 requiring their ongoing payment. Mr [R’s] evidence appears to be that he has ceased to make those payments or intends to stop making those payments.
Where Mr [R] is the sole Director of [C Pty Ltd Entity 5] and [C Pty Ltd Entity 4] and he gives evidence that he is proposing a moratorium on repayments for a loan the Respondent Wife is liable for the Respondent says he must be joined to prevent dissipation of the asset pool and to ensure the enforceability of any orders made by the court with respect to either the payment of that loan or the discharge of the Respondent Wife’s liability for that loan which will be sought on a final basis.
Whilst the 20 March 2026 Affidavit does not refer to Mr [R’s] role as Director of [C Pty Ltd Entity 2], the 484 Form completed and Annexed to the Applicant Husband’s Affidavit of 17 February 2026 and the Affidavit signed by Mr [R] and annexed to the Applicant Husband’s Affidavit on that date indicate he was director at that time. Specific orders are sought with respect to Mr [R’s] role as director of [C Pty Ltd Entity 2] and with respect to [C Pty Ltd Entity 2] more generally in the Application in a Proceeding which will impinge upon his rights.

Mr T – Shareholder and Director of [C Pty Ltd Entity 3]
[J Pty Ltd], of which the Respondent Wife is the director and sole shareholder, previously held 900 of the 1,125 issued shares in [C Pty Ltd Entity 3]. Those shares were transferred to [C Pty Ltd Entity 3] on [...] March 2025. The remaining shares are held by “[BB] Pty Ltd”, the husband advised the Single Expert that the beneficial owner of those shares is the [CC] Family Trust, being “[T’s] Family Trust”.
Orders are sought under section 106B with respect to the change in shareholding which will impact upon Mr [T] as a fellow shareholder.
In the event that liabilities of the marriage attached to the businesses, namely the [AA Finance Company] Loans with [C Pty Ltd Entity 4] and [C Pty Ltd Entity 5], or the [DD Bank] Loan with [C Pty Ltd Entity 2], cannot be properly discharged or transferred the Respondent Wife proposes the sale of shareholdings. The shareholders agreements provided with respect to [C Pty Ltd Entity 4], [C Pty Ltd Entity 5] and [C Pty Ltd Entity 2] provide a process for sale of shareholdings (noting a shareholders agreement with respect to [C Pty Ltd Entity 3] has not been disclosed). The process for disposal of shareholdings is outlined in [C Pty Ltd Entity 2] Shareholders agreement which is annexed to the Applicant Husband’s Affidavit of 16 February 2026 and requires consent of all shareholders, which includes both Mr [T] for [C Pty Ltd Entity 3] and Mr [R] for [C Pty Ltd Entity 4] and [C Pty Ltd Entity 5].

(Footnotes omitted)
The husband, for his part, seeks on a final basis a declaration that Mr U holds the whole of his interest in Property 1 on a resulting and/or constructive trust for the wife, and a declaration that Mrs V holds the whole of her interest in Property 2 on a resulting and/or constructing trust for the wife.
Beyond seeking those declarations, the husband does not seek any further remedial orders in relation to those properties, and his application on a final basis seeks dismissal of the wife’s relief with no adjustive orders.
The husband’s affidavit contends that Property 2 is registered in the name of Mrs V and Property 1 is registered in the name of Mr U. He further contends that the wife signed a document on 12 January 2021 transferring a property she owned in Suburb N by way of gift to her mother. The husband says that the property was subsequently sold, and the proceeds of sale were used to purchase the above properties.
Each of the proposed respondents, Mrs V and Mr U, asserts in their affidavits that there is no basis for the joinder applications relating to them.

APPLICABLE LAW

Section 79AA(10)(b) of the Act provides that any person whose interests would be affected by an order is entitled to be joined to the proceedings.
The Act reflects what McHugh J observed in Victoria v Sutton [1998] HCA 56; (1998) 195 CLR 291:
The rules of natural justice require that, before a court makes an order that may affect the rights or interests of a person, that person should be given an opportunity to contest the making of that order. Because that is so, it is the invariable practice of the courts to require such a person to be joined as a party if there is an arguable possibility that he or she may be affected by the making of the order. ...
(Footnote omitted)
Rule 3.01 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”) provides:
3.01 Necessary parties

A person whose rights may be directly affected by an issue in a proceeding, and whose participation as a party is necessary for the court to determine all issues in dispute in the proceeding, must be included as a party to the proceeding.
Rule 3.03(4) of the Rules provides that a party who seeks to join a third party to the proceedings after the first court date must seek leave to do so.
In Wayne v Dillon [2008] FamCAFC 204; (2008) 40 Fam LR 543 (Wayne v Dillon), Warnick J observed, in respect of the superseded Family Law Rules 2004 (Cth), that “necessary” meant:
... something more than “useful” or “expeditious”. In my view, if there are available alternative means to joinder to the substantive proceedings, of obtaining from a third person or someone already a party what is needed to allow an applicant for joinder to establish an identified “case”, joinder is unlikely to be “necessary”.
However, if a cause of action, recognisable at law, against a “third person” is particularised, then it is at least highly likely that joinder will be “necessary for the court to completely and finally determine all matters in dispute”. ...

DISCUSSION

In relation to the application brought by the wife, I accept that the wife’s submissions referred to above establish that the rights of the parties she seeks to be joined to the proceedings may be directly affected by the orders she seeks. Consequently, I am satisfied that it is necessary for the Court to join those persons and companies to the proceedings in order to finally determine all matters in dispute (see Wayne v Dillon (supra)).
The joinder of these parties, however, comes at significant peril to the wife. If she does not make good the relief that she seeks against each of the proposed parties, by failing to discharge the onus of proof she carries at the final hearing, she may reasonably anticipate an application for costs, including potentially on an indemnity basis
I am nevertheless satisfied, at the relatively low threshold required to establish necessity for joinder, that the wife has discharged that obligation.
I am not satisfied that the husband’s application for joinder has any merit. On a final basis, the husband seeks no orders that would affect the ownership of the properties about which he seeks a declaration. It remains open to him to contend at trial that those properties are held on behalf of the wife. In circumstances where he seeks no order in relation to the properties, there is no utility in the joinder application.

DISPOSITION

An order will be made joining the following parties to the proceedings: Mr R as the fourth respondent, Mr T as the seventh respondent, C Pty Ltd Entity 3 as the eighth respondent, C Pty Ltd Entity 4 as the ninth respondent and C Pty Ltd Entity 5 as the tenth Respondent.
An order will be made dismissing the husband’s Application in a Proceeding filed on 6 March 2026.
